story:
Detective Varo had worked plenty of ugly scenes, but never a call about dead birds. A trail of ravens lay twisted in the frost-black mud, eyes open, legs snapped at odd angles. Alia, the new partner with lilac on her skin and a smile that softened his judgment, urged him to wait for backup. He waved her off. He felt off-kilter—noticed, even, that his spare magazines weren’t loaded, a mistake he’d never made. He went in anyway.

He followed the path of carcasses into a dark clearing. A figure waited there, from head to boots in black, a plague doctor mask tilted toward him. Varo keyed his radio. Alia’s voice murmured, “Right behind you, sir.” The wind shifted and the smell of rot hit the back of his throat. He stepped closer, tried a joke, got nothing back. Two yards. A footstep in the brush. A blow like a hammer. Lilac, and then the dark.

He woke to a throbbing skull in a concrete cell barely wider than his arm span. One naked bulb. A bolted chair. Rope biting his ribs. The door opened on a long groan and the masked figure came in carrying a grain sack stained a deep, rusted black. Varo bluffed—federal agent, big mistake to kidnap him. The figure’s shoulders shook in a soundless laugh. The sack opened. The stench hit like a wave. He retched until he shook. Inside: ravens by the dozens, hacked and bled. A cloth pressed to his face. The dark again.

When the world returned, the ropes were gone, replaced by something worse. Feathers—oil-black with a purple sheen—covered him. Every inch. His hands were not hands, but clawed, half-human things with just enough grip to make the change feel obscene. He screamed and clawed at himself until he had no breath left. Then he saw the wall hook that had held nothing before. On it hung a black robe, the same beaked mask, a folded paper in the pocket.

Sorry, the note said in a hurried scrawl. Thought I was just meeting a girl for drinks. Mira—or whatever the woman with you calls herself—set me up. Made me into what you are now. You’ve got a year to collect those ravens and pass them on to someone else so they become like us. Fail, and you’re stuck. Get to it.

The paper slid from his feathered fingers to the floor. The lilac. The way Alia had materialized behind him in the trees. The feeling he’d ignored in his teeth whenever she was near. It all landed at once: the case wasn’t about birds—it was a trap. Alia, Mira, whoever she was, had guided him to the clearing and handed him over. The mask wasn’t a costume. It was a job. A sentence. A chain that ran backward through men who’d thought they were just meeting someone for a drink.

He tried to tear the feathers out until his skin bled. He sobbed, then went quiet. He stood. He put on the robe, then the mask, its beak heavy and hollow with other people’s breath. His clawed hand found the corroded brass knob. For a heartbeat he paused, the old instinct inside him screaming to turn back. Then he twisted, opened the door, and stepped into the night to follow the trail he’d once tried to solve—now the one he had to lay.
